era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV, mid-reign period. Khufu or his successor (possibly Khafre) was ruling. Monumental construction continued. In Mesopotamia, the Akkad Empire was consolidating (though Sargon's reign is traditionally dated c. 2334–2279 BCE, placing this year in the pre-Akkadian Sumerian city-state period). The Indus Valley Civilisation was in its early urban phase. This year falls within the era of centralised Egyptian pharaonic power and monumental architecture.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesopotamia — no specific events documented for this year
Indus Valley — chronology and specific events poorly attested
China — Xia Dynasty traditionally dated here; no contemporary written records
Sub-Saharan Africa, Americas, Oceania — no written records

events.title: Continuation of Giza pyramid complex construction and Old Kingdom administrative consolidation
events.description: During this year, the Giza pyramid complex remained under construction or in its final phases. The administrative apparatus of Dynasty IV continued to function, managing labour, resources, and religious ceremonies. No specific events are documented for this year; the entry represents the broader context of Old Kingdom stability and monumental ambition. The pharaonic state maintained tight control over the Nile Valley, extracting surplus for pyramid construction and maintaining a centralised bureaucracy.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza and Memphis (stratigraphy, artefacts)
Administrative records and inscriptions from Old Kingdom tombs

events.certainty_note: No specific events are attested for this year. The entry reflects the broader era context. Absolute dating remains uncertain (±50–100 years).

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 2613 BCE, so no disconfirming evidence can be assessed. The broader context of Dynasty IV stability is not disputed, but the absolute chronology remains contested (see -2614 entry).
historiographic_note: For this year, the historical record is essentially silent. No contemporary written records, inscriptions, or chronicles document specific events. The entry is based entirely on archaeological context and the assumption of continuity in Old Kingdom administration. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; it reflects the scarcity of surviving records from this period and the difficulty of assigning specific events to specific years in the 26th century BCE.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the same era and construction programme
